title: The Realization Effect: Risk-Taking after Realized versus Paper Losses

paper_tests.result_description: Participants who realized their losses decreased investment in round 4, while those with paper losses increased investment.
Non-parametric confirmation: paper loss group invests significantly more than realized loss group in round 4.

description: Imas (2016) investigates the "realization effect" — whether the way prior losses are experienced changes subsequent risk-taking behavior. Participants receive an endowment and play multiple rounds of a lottery investment game, choosing how much to allocate to a positively skewed gamble in each round. The key manipulation occurs after participants experience a loss: in the *paper loss* condition, losses remain unrealized on paper, while in the *realized loss* condition, participants' accounts are settled and they receive a fresh endowment before continuing. The outcome is the investment level in the final round, which captures how prior loss framing shapes willingness to take financial risk. The original study finds a striking divergence: participants carrying paper losses increase their risk-taking — consistent with loss-chasing behavior — while those whose losses were realized become significantly more conservative, investing less in the subsequent round.

title: What You See Is All There Is

paper_tests.result_description: Subjects in the Selected treatment exhibit substantial correlation neglect (naivete ~0.48), while Control subjects show near-Bayesian updating (~-0.09).
Mann-Whitney confirmation: Selected treatment has significantly higher naivete than Control.

Mann-Whitney confirmation: Selected treatment has significantly higher naivete than Control.
Subjects in the Selected treatment exhibit substantial correlation neglect (naivete ~0.48), while Control subjects show near-Bayesian updating (~-0.09).
Mann-Whitney confirmation: Selected treatment has significantly higher naivete than Control.

description: Enke (2020) investigates whether people neglect the selection process behind the information they observe — a phenomenon related to Kahneman's "what you see is all there is" heuristic. Participants are asked to estimate an unknown numerical state based on a set of signals, first forming a preliminary judgment from a single signal and then updating their belief after seeing additional signals from an information source. The key manipulation is how those additional signals are chosen: in the Selected treatment, the source strategically picks signals that confirm the participant's initial guess, while in the Control treatment, signals are drawn at random — meaning participants should, in principle, account for the source's selection bias when updating. The outcome is a naivete parameter capturing how much each participant's belief updating deviates from the Bayesian benchmark, where higher values indicate greater neglect of the selection process. The original study finds that participants in the Selected treatment exhibit substantial correlation neglect — updating their beliefs almost as if the strategically chosen signals were random — while Control participants update in a near-Bayesian manner, confirming that people systematically fail to account for why they are seeing the information they see.

description: Enke and Graeber (2023) investigate how cognitive uncertainty — people's subjective confidence in their own valuations — systematically distorts decisions under risk. Participants on Prolific are presented with simple binary lotteries that pay a dollar amount with some probability and nothing otherwise, and they state the certain dollar amount they would accept instead of playing each lottery — their certainty equivalent — using an incentive-compatible BDM mechanism. The key manipulation varies the probability of winning across conditions, ranging from very low to very high, allowing the authors to trace out how valuations deviate from expected value across the probability spectrum. After each valuation, participants also report their cognitive uncertainty — how confident they are that their stated value is close to their true preference — providing a direct measure of decision noise. The outcome is the normalized certainty equivalent — the stated value divided by the lottery prize — which captures whether participants overweight or underweight probabilities relative to their actuarially fair values. The original study finds the classic probability weighting pattern: at low probabilities participants substantially overvalue the lottery, while at high probabilities they substantially undervalue it, and both distortions are strongly predicted by self-reported cognitive uncertainty.

description: Enke and Graeber (2023) investigate how cognitive uncertainty — a person's subjective sense of confusion about what they believe — distorts probabilistic reasoning. Participants complete a series of balls-and-urns updating tasks: two bags each contain a known mix of red and blue balls, the computer randomly selects one bag and draws a sample, and participants state their probabilistic belief that a specific bag was chosen. The key manipulation varies the difficulty of the updating problem, producing cases where the correct Bayesian posterior is either low (around 15%) or high (around 85%), which lets the authors test whether stated beliefs systematically deviate from rational benchmarks. The outcome is the stated posterior belief, which captures whether people compress their probability judgments toward 50% when the reasoning is hard. The original study finds that beliefs are indeed compressed: participants overestimate when the true posterior is low and underestimate when it is high, consistent with cognitive uncertainty pushing people toward a mental default of maximum uncertainty.

paper_tests.result_description: Adding a zero has no effect in Charity/Charity (neutral condition), confirming the effect is driven by motivated reasoning.

description: Exley and Kessler (2024) investigate whether people make systematic errors in evaluating charitable giving when they have a self-interested reason to do so — a phenomenon they call "motivated reasoning." Participants on Amazon Mechanical Turk make a series of binary choices between an outside option and a "bundle" — a sum of several small amounts donated to the Make-A-Wish Foundation. The key manipulation is whether adding a zero-valued amount to a bundle changes how often participants choose it: in the Self/Charity condition, the outside option is money for the participant themselves, giving them a selfish motive to reject the charitable bundle, while in the Charity/Charity condition, both options benefit charity, removing any self-interested motive. The outcome is the "zero effect" — the change in bundle choice rates when a worthless zero is added — which captures whether people use irrelevant information as an excuse to act selfishly. The original study finds that adding a zero to a charity bundle reduces bundle choice by about 6 percentage points when participants have a selfish motive, but has no effect when both options benefit charity, confirming that the errors are driven by motivated reasoning rather than genuine confusion.

description: Exley and Kessler (2024) investigate whether people's tendency to neglect correlation in information sources is influenced by whether doing so serves their preferences. Participants receive a known estimate and three news channels — all correlated because they share a common component — and must predict a target number equal to the average of four underlying estimates. Across conditions, participants are given incentives to either overestimate or underestimate the target, or they face neutral incentives in the control condition. The outcome is the mean estimation error, which captures the degree to which participants over-weight correlated signals in a direction that aligns with their motivated goals. The original study finds a striking asymmetry: participants in the overestimate condition exhibit significantly more correlation neglect — producing larger negative errors — than those in the control condition, while participants in the underestimate condition show less correlation neglect, confirming that the bias is not purely cognitive but is amplified or dampened by directional motivation.

description: Exley and Kessler (2024) investigate whether people exhibit larger anchoring bias when doing so aligns with their financial incentives — a form of "motivated error." Participants answer trivia questions after being shown a random anchor, and they are paid based on accuracy, incentivized to overestimate, or incentivized to underestimate. The key manipulation is whether the direction of the anchoring bias — pulling estimates toward the anchor — happens to coincide with the direction that earns the participant more money. The outcome is mean estimation error, which captures the degree to which participants' answers are systematically pulled toward the anchor beyond what pure cognitive bias would predict. The original study finds a striking asymmetry: participants in the overestimate condition — where anchoring bias and financial incentives align — show significantly larger errors than control, while participants in the underestimate condition — where bias and incentives conflict — show no difference from control, suggesting people selectively succumb to bias when it serves their interests.

description: Exley and Kessler (2024) test whether anchoring bias in numerical estimation is amplified when errors serve participants' self-interest — a phenomenon they call "motivated errors." MTurk workers answer trivia questions after seeing an anchor value, but unlike a standard anchoring design, they must first guess whether the true answer is above or below the anchor before entering their estimate on a slider defaulting to 50 rather than the anchor itself. Across three conditions — overestimate, underestimate, and control — the key manipulation is whether being anchored toward an incorrect answer would increase or decrease the participant's bonus payment, creating a financial incentive to be biased in a particular direction. The outcome is mean estimation error, which captures the degree to which participants' guesses are pulled toward the anchor and whether that pull is stronger when self-serving. The original study finds that even with the direction-first design and neutral slider starting point — modifications that reduce overall anchoring — participants in the overestimate condition show significantly larger bias than control, while those in the underestimate condition show less, confirming that motivated reasoning persists even when procedural safeguards against anchoring are introduced.
